speaker: interviewer
text: Number two, survivor is Benjamin Jacobs. The date is July 16, 1996. We left off, on the last tape you were discussing a play that you were in, where the father came home. And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the father was upset, obviously, terribly upset, and so was the mother. Because they felt that now that daughter at best can only be a divorcee, because she had-- have to have a real divorce. Otherwise, she would have to live with this man. And he was quite below their status. And so he asked the man to come back in the morning, and he'll straighten everything out. And that came the second act, when he comes back in the morning. And the father sits down with this poor [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] halfwit, so to speak. And he offers him money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he says, I don't want money. I don't need money. What do you want? Well, I want to be married to your daughter. Now that they married me, I don't want to get a divorce.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course they were all besides themself. They didn't know what to do. They cannot force this man to divorce his daughter. But they can also not allow him this daughter to live with him. And so as time went on, the daughter came in, and she started to listen to this man. And there were several scenes beyond this, in which she became more acquainted with him as a person, rather than as the halfwit that the town saw him [BACKROUND NOISE] and saw his real wisdom, his character, and his being as being a real fine person. And then she in the last act insists that her parents allow her to be married to the man. And that is such a wonderful ending, such a nice ending, where rich and poor see one another not through the money and not through the riches but see one another through the soul and through the heart that they have. A wonderful play. I can't remember the name of it. But I can really remember the whole dialogue yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: What schools did you attend before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I attended the Dobra Public School. And then I attended the Jewish gymnasium in Kalisz and a dental Institute in Kalisz beyond this for one year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall how some of the holidays were celebrated in your home, the Jewish holidays? What's your favorite Jewish holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, my favorite Jewish holiday was Tisha B'Av. Because on Tisha B'Av, we could throw these little things, these little things that grow on bushes that stick to the clothes. I don't know what they're called in English. [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thorns?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had some [NON-ENGLISH]. What is it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thorns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thorns, that's right. And this was a child's game. We could throw them, and we could not be regarded as someone unruly doing this, because this was the sort of thing that we did. And of course I liked very much after [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Passover, where we had nuts in temple, where we throw nuts over the Torahs, and everyone got [NON-ENGLISH]. This was something very special for me. Our holidays were very, very well-observed, that is totally observed. My father would take us by our hands, lead us to the temple. We'd sit around him, and he would have time for us at that point. And he would put his hand on our heads and put his-- do his blessings and sort of show his real love, which he did not have much time to show during other times. And so did my mother when we came home from shul. She would ask how shul was. And she would sit separately from my father, upstairs, she could not sit with my father. This was not allowed in those days. And so these were the precious days that I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you celebrate a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a smaller bar mitzvah. But my brother had a very, very pompous bar mitzvah, in which he was then a [NON-ENGLISH]. He was supposed to go to yeshiva, and he was supposed to be a very Orthodox, religious, pious student after yeshiva. He changed his mind obviously, afterwards. But I remember the rabbi was forecasting that he is going to be one of the town's great yeshiva [NON-ENGLISH]. And, but my bar mitzvah, when I was bar mitzvahed, it was already during the times that Hitler has already spread, spilt his venom over the border to Poland. And Poland already emulated Hitler's pogroms and Hitler's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [? braving ?] of Jews and so forth. And my bar mitzvah was just very small in temple, where I was given an aliyah in my bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe the Jewish community in Dobra? Were there many synagogues or just one synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there were four synagogues in a small town like Dobra for 150 families. That was only about 300, 400, 400 souls, and there were four synagogues. And there was rivalry beyond, beyond explanation. On some holidays, there was blood running on the, on the-- between one shtiebel and the other shtiebel in the temple. My father was a member of the large temple, which was the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] temple that most Jews were-- I would say medium well-to-do Jews. The rest of them were divided into three different groups. One was the [INAUDIBLE] shtiebel. That was a shtiebel rather than a shul. It was a small room, in which a number, like 40 or 50, would meet in the vicinity of the temple. It was all in one particular yard. And the next one was the Alexander [INAUDIBLE], which was also separate. It was more progressive, not quite as orthodox as the [INAUDIBLE]. And then there was a overflow of crowd from the main temple that could not afford to buy seats. There was an extra hall for those people as well, like an auditorium that we call here. To characterize it as which one was conservative and which one was reformed, it's very difficult. Because conservative here means something different than conservative 50 or 60 or 70 years ago. In those days, there was no such thing as reformed. You were either conservative or hasidic or ultra hassidic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other Jewish institutions in Dobra-- Jewish newspaper, Jewish-- you mentioned Jewish theater. Was there anything else?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was, there were only the organizations like Halutz, [? Halutzim, ?] Zionist organizations, and there were labor organizations and obviously Communists as well. But to say that there should have been some educational facilities beyond high-- beyond public school, Dobra was too small for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of relationship did the Jews have with the non-Jews of the village, what-- before the occupation. Did they enjoy good relationships with the non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say that as long as Marshal Pilsudski was the president of Poland. He was the signatory to a special document, as a matter of fact, that was mandated by the United States at the League of Nations, when Poland became-- got independence, Poland became a state. They had to sign a separate mandate, in which they have proclaimed to give equal rights to minorities in Poland. And so as long as Marshal Pilsudski was the president, there was a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] polite [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but not completely tolerable relationship between non-Jews and Jews. Something was always in the way of their Roman Catholic scriptures and the Roman Catholic [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] fathers that have priests-- the priests that have preached in their churches that was not always allowed them to accept Jews. They lived too long with the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] out-aged and outlived [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] kind of enigma that existed against Jews. And so it was polite, and it was quasi not obvious and not overt. But it was there. I, as a child, did not feel it. I played with Christian children, with Roman Catholics, with, with Protestants, with Germans, with any other-- Russians. And I did not feel it. However, when I at the age of 13, when Hitler took power in 1933, things changed. And I was at many times exposed to certain intimidations, stories that were so anti-Semitic that I could not stomach them. I came home and I asked my parents whether this is true. And they had a hard time to explain me what is the difference between truth and the difference between people making up truths. That is the time when shortly thereafter, Pilsudksi died and Rydz-Smigly became the president. And when Rydz-Smigly became the president, the right [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] became more excessive to Rydz-Smigly-- Rydz-Smigly made them more excessive to himself. And so the accent changed. The character of Jewish-Catholic Polish relations changed very much. A Jew was not seen as a, as a Polish person. He was seen as a Jew. Even so he was a citizen, he was not regarded as being a citizen. Because they felt that in order to be a Polish citizen, you must be Christian first.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did life really begin to change for you? What year would you say you began to notice?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say the very year that I mentioned, 1933. It does not take long for the Polish right to pick up on, pick up on Hitler's ideas. And so they emulated him. They invited-- as a matter of fact, shortly before the war, before the war, they invited Goring for a, for a hunt in Polish woods and played to the Germans, up as best as they could. They're hoping that they can avoid a war with Germany. But it was two sided, sort of. It wasn't just the war. It was that they really were persuaded by Hitler's seeing the Jews as the evil of Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you personally--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they demand our dispossession. They demanded our [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] emigration from Poland. They always, their papers, their newspapers always proclaimed that there are too many Jews in Poland, too much influence in Poland. The old story that although we had no Jewish teachers in public school, only in the Yiddish schools were there Jewish teachers, yet they always insisted there were no Jewish people working for the government, very, very few. None at all that I know. Yet they always lived upon this nuance that Jews have too much influence in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your life personally begin to change as-- as Hitler rose to power in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: My life began to change in the last year of public school, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because at that point, we were very often persecuted by non-Jewish children, by non-Jewish youngsters my age. During recession, we had to assemble in one spot of the yard. And in-- and in view of the teachers, so we would not be accosted and beaten and kicked and spat at and things like that. Even walking from that point to the men's rooms, to the washrooms, was a trauma. Because on the way, you could have been-- you could have been misused.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is where it really changed for me. From that point on, my parents decided that I ought to go to a Yiddish school. And so they send me to college, to the Yiddish gymnasium, Jewish gymnasium, so called. And-- and what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1934.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you-- did your parents ever discuss the thought of, of leaving--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: They never thought of, of changing, of leaving Poland. They felt that this is something that Hitler is just making a lot of noise. And that once he is elected and once he has, he has established power and that he will not be as, as venomous as he was barking about. As they say, the dog barks louder than he bites. And that is what generally they believe in the Jewish community, also in Germany, as well as in Poland and as well as in the other Eastern and central European countries. They believe that once Hitler has gathered enough power and enough security himself and that he will not use the Jews as a, as a sounding block, so to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Dobra occupied?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dobra was occupied the next day of 9-- of the war, in September 2 or September 3 of 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing, when, that day, when the town was occupied. Do you have any recollections, personal recollections?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do, I do. Before, before, before-- a few days before the war, my brother, who was in the cavalry, was called back to service. And I felt that-- and I always felt that way when I was younger as well-- that living in Poland, I ought to live like a Pole. I ought to feel [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] solidarity with the Polish people, whatever Polish people feel like, even so that I was Jewish. And what I mean to say by this is, is, Louise, that I felt that I am Jewish, but I am a Pole. Unlike we were looked upon that we were not Poles, we were just Jews-- we couldn't be Poles-- I felt that if I perhaps speak Polish, act as a Pole, do more national things, observe national holidays and even enlist in the army, that I would be looked upon as a equal, as a Pole. But I was not accepted. I felt solidarity with my brother perhaps, as much as with the war itself. I went to, to register. And they sent me back, because I was only 20-- I was not quite 21. It was September. November, I would have been 21. So the recruiter send me back, because 21 was the minimum age to recruit in those days, to even volunteer in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was sent back. And then things took a turn for the unexplainable. We were bombarded. We were made to believe that if we escape to the other side of the Warta River, which was only about 40 kilometers from us, east, there is where the Poles will take a stand to resist the Germans. And we'll fight them back. We'll beat them back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as we left with our wagon and a few things on the wagon, and we were on the road. And the German Stukas were coming down and bombing the road and bombing the civilians. Our horses were scared and went down the embankment, and the grass embankment and the wagon-- with all our belongings, we were barely able to jump off the wagon-- plunged into the river with the horses.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so we were there, with thousands of people streaming by us, nobody caring. Everyone was concerned with his own life. Until finally, one of the very big landowners that my father used to deal with, Mr. Chmielinski, came by. And he said, Awigdor, what are you doing here? And he ran next to his wagon as he was moving, and he told him the story. And he invited us to come with him. So we went-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so when we were bombarded again. And then we found that our safe traveling time would be at night rather than during the day. We traveled at night, until we came to the [INAUDIBLE], which is west of Warsaw. And there the Germans overran us, and we came back home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. Chmielinski was within two weeks arrested, as most of the intelligentsia were, and the rich Polish people. And his wife and son received his remains in a little box, the ashes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did your life change? You came back home, and then what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were proclamations and directives and laws that Jews are not allowed to do this and Jews are not allowed to do that. Jews are not allowed to leave this town. Jews are-- there was a curfew at 7 o'clock-- only for Jews, not for Polish people. They were making quite a distinction between Jews and Poles. What they prohibited Jews, they still allowed Poles. And with that, they wanted to get the support of Poles. They did not want to antagonize the Polish people, so they could have them-- use them, so to speak, to possibly-- as instruments of destruction of their Jewish neighbors. And so they created a sort of a wall of understanding between Jews and non-Jews to the extreme. They drove this so far, that Polish people were pointing out to them where Jews lived, so they could go in and they could plunder and they could beat and they could do whatever they wanted. And this is how it started. My best friend, Szymon Treskawer, who went with me through school, my age. I remember he was only a few days apart from my day of birth, as we often compared. Also a member of the dramatic circle. He was hung in the center of town, just a few days before I even came back from Warsaw, we came back from Warsaw. He was hung with 10 other citizens in Poland. Not only Jews. They just picked one, one, and one, and one, and one. This was supposed to be a sort of a reminder that they deal with people in a tough manner, that anyone who has any idea to resist or go any other way or think any other way would end up at the gallows. And the whole city was supposed-- was kept cruising around this circle, where these 10 people hung for hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name of the leader, the officer, that was in charge when your town was occupied, the German commandant? Was there a particular person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this, this was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] done by the first army people. They weren't SS men. They were just army people. I don't remember any one of them, because they moved on subsequently and others came, an other gendarmerie came. Then at that point, they had a [GERMAN] so-called. They had a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what we would call here a administrator of the area, a German. And I have his name in the book, but I don't remember his name at this point. It escape me, it's been so many years. He was the administrator of the Dobra region. He then took over all of the laws and the proclamations and the directives to Jews that they should give up the radios, they should give up any valuables that they had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and all of those things. Resettlements, driving out from, from your home and bringing into a ghetto. All of this was under this particular [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you next? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were driven out our home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And sent where?

speaker: interviewee
text: And sent across the street to a school, which wasn't operated, into the attic, in one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And our-- our home, our land, our belonging, we could only take what we could take in our two hands, in our front of our chest.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take personally? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I took some books. I took some personal things of my-- photographs, little bit of clothing, I remember one suit that I had been made for me for that particular Rosh Hashanah and a couple of just ordinary, ordinary things that I would feel that are necessary as a first need. And the home and the land and the furniture and everything that we had was given to a Volksdeutsche. Volksdeutsche were Germans that were living in, in Poland. They were left over, after the First World War. And he became the owner of all of that. And we looked down from the attic across the street and saw him using our home. It was very painful for my mother and my father. My father was an optimist. My mother was more of a pragmatist. My father said, oh, it won't take long. It will be over in a year, and we'll go back to our house, be like it was before. It never was, Louise. It never was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other families with you in this attic? Or was it just your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just our family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the living quarters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a large room, where I believe the supplies were kept of the school. And my mother put some curtains around, to provide the minimum kind of a privacy for herself and for the children and for ourself. And that's how we lived. It was small and, it was all that we could have.

speaker: interviewer
text: What you do while you were there? Were you working at the time, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Working for a Jew was not allowed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was tending a little garden in back of the school. And it wasn't very long, because we were only there for about six months, probably from about September through next April.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we were ordered into a ghetto, in the center of town, in the poorest section of Dobra. That's where the Jews were brought into, encircled, fenced in, got it, ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know who was in charge of this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the same, the same administrator, whose name I'll look up and probably give you on the next roll.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you entered the ghetto in the spring, did you say, of 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in this ghetto till May, 1941, approximately a year, a little bit over a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were assigned to another house, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were assigned to a room of a house, without a floor, with just dirt floor, worse than what we were in the schoolhouse.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop this tape. And we will continue with the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number three, shortly.
